content.body: Polymers are large molecules made of repeating monomer units. They are found in nature (proteins, DNA, cellulose) and are synthesised industrially (plastics, fibres).

Addition polymerisation:
nCH₂=CH₂ → (-CH₂-CH₂-)ₙ  (poly(ethene))

Condensation polymerisation:
Monomer₁ + Monomer₂ → Polymer + small molecule (H₂O or HCl)

Nylon (polyamide): amine + carboxylic acid → amide bond + H₂O
PET (polyester): diol + dicarboxylic acid → ester bond + H₂O

Biodegradable polymers: Polylactic acid (PLA), PHB

content.title: Addition vs Condensation
content.body:
Addition: All atoms in monomers end up in polymer
Poly(ethene): CH₂=CH₂ → (-CH₂CH₂-)ₙ

Condensation: Small molecule lost per bond formed
Nylon-6,6: H₂N-(CH₂)₆-NH₂ + HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH
→ (-NH-(CH₂)₆-NH-CO-(CH₂)₄-CO-)ₙ + H₂O

questions.question: Addition polymerisation requires monomers with:
questions.explanation: Addition polymers form from alkene monomers whose double bonds open to join together

questions.question: Condensation polymerisation always produces a polymer and:
questions.explanation: Condensation reactions eliminate a small molecule (H₂O or HCl) for each bond formed

questions.question: Nylon is a type of:
questions.explanation: Nylon contains amide (-CO-NH-) linkages, making it a polyamide

questions.question: Which polymer is biodegradable?
questions.explanation: PLA is derived from corn starch and breaks down in composting conditions

questions.question: The repeating unit in poly(propene) is:
questions.explanation: Poly(propene) is made from propene (CH₂=CHCH₃), giving the repeat unit -CH₂-CH(CH₃)-

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

lessons.title: Types of Polymers and their Properties

content.body:
Addition polymerisation:
nCH₂=CH₂ → (−CH₂−CH₂−)ₙ
Monomers with C=C double bonds join together
No atoms lost
Examples: poly(ethene), poly(propene), PVC, PTFE

Condensation polymerisation:
Monomers with two functional groups react
Small molecule (H₂O or HCl) released each time
Examples: nylon (polyamide), polyester, proteins, DNA

content.title: Key Polymers
content.body:
Poly(ethene) — packaging, plastic bags (flexible)
Poly(propene) — ropes, containers (stronger)
PVC — pipes, window frames (rigid)
Nylon — clothing, ropes (strong, polyamide)
Polyester (Terylene) — clothing, PET bottles
Kevlar — bullet-proof vests, polyaramide

Thermoplastics soften on heating (can be remoulded — recycled). Thermosetting polymers are permanently cross-linked — do not soften on heating. Biodegradable polymers break down naturally; most synthetic polymers are persistent environmental pollutants.

questions.question: Addition polymerisation requires monomers with...
questions.explanation: Addition polymerisation uses alkene monomers. The double bond opens and monomers join in a chain with no atoms lost.

questions.question: In condensation polymerisation, what small molecule is released?
questions.explanation: Condensation polymerisation releases small molecules like water (from polyesters and polyamides) or HCl.

questions.question: Nylon is an example of a...
questions.explanation: Nylon is formed by condensation polymerisation between diamines and dicarboxylic acids, forming amide (−CONH−) linkages.

questions.question: PET (polyester) bottles are made by condensation of...
questions.explanation: PET (polyethylene terephthalate) is a polyester made from ethane-1,2-diol and benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid.

questions.question: A thermoplastic polymer...
options: Softens and can be remoulded when heated
questions.answer: Softens and can be remoulded when heated
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics have no cross-links between chains, so they soften on heating and can be reshaped and recycled.

questions.question: Kevlar is used in bullet-proof vests because...
options: It has very strong hydrogen bonding between chains making it tough
questions.answer: It has very strong hydrogen bonding between chains making it tough
questions.explanation: Kevlar chains are aligned and connected by strong hydrogen bonds between amide groups, giving exceptional strength and toughness.

questions.question: The repeat unit of poly(ethene) is...
questions.explanation: The repeat unit in a polymer is what repeats n times. For poly(ethene) made from CH₂=CH₂: repeat unit is −(CH₂CH₂)−

questions.question: Most synthetic polymers are an environmental problem because...
options: They do not break down naturally
questions.answer: They do not break down naturally
questions.explanation: Synthetic polymers like polyethene are non-biodegradable. They persist in the environment for hundreds of years.

questions.question: Proteins are natural examples of...
questions.explanation: Proteins are natural polyamides — amino acids join by peptide bonds (−CO−NH−) in condensation reactions, releasing water.

questions.question: What type of polymer cannot be remoulded when heated?
questions.explanation: Thermosetting polymers have permanent cross-links formed during curing. These prevent softening — they will char rather than melt.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Polymers. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Polymers concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Polymers builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Polymers. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Polymers. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Polymers include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Polymers. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
